source_title: Study of Impact of Telehealth Use on Clinic No Show Rates at an Academic Practice
population_setting: East Carolina University academic psychiatry clinic; 4,650 telehealth appointments during COVID and 3,843 face-to-face appointments pre-COVID
statistic: Overall no-show rate declined from 18.1% pre-telehealth to 15.3% telehealth. For new visits: face-to-face 29.8%, telephone 7.7%, video 29.8%. For return visits: face-to-face 16.0%, telephone 7.8%, video 17.1%.
use_in_model: Supports modeling first-visit completion and no-show sensitivity. Shows telehealth can reduce no-shows, but video may not outperform face-to-face in this setting while telephone was much lower friction.
limitation: Single academic site. COVID-period workflows may not generalize. Telephone visits may differ from video visits in clinical requirements and patient effort.
notes: Strong psychiatry-specific benchmark. Useful for booked-visit-to-completed-visit assumptions.

category: Telehealth vs in-person no-show rates in rural behavioral health
source_title: Examining differences in time to appointment and no-show rates between rural telehealth users and non-users
source_type: Peer-reviewed digital health study / Frontiers in Digital Health
population_setting: US rural health system; more than 9,000 appointments across behavioral health, endocrinology, and other specialties
statistic: For return behavioral-health visits, remote telehealth no-show rate was 11.5% versus 16.1% in-person. Odds ratio 0.68, p = 0.004. For new visits, 6.36% remote versus 10.64% in-person was not statistically significant.
use_in_model: Supports modeling no-show reduction for telehealth and follow-up visits. Shows a lower no-show rate for return behavioral-health telehealth visits.
limitation: Includes multiple specialties. Behavioral-health results are a subset. New-visit difference was not statistically significant.
notes: Useful as a behavioral-health telehealth proxy, especially for ongoing attendance assumptions.

source_title: Telemedicine appointments are more likely to be completed than in-person healthcare appointments: a retrospective cohort study
source_type: Peer-reviewed retrospective cohort / JAMIA Open / PMC
population_setting: Two large healthcare organizations; more than 1.7 million appointments across multiple specialties, including some mental health
metric: Appointment completion, cancellation, and no-show rates
statistic: Completion rate was 73.4% for telemedicine versus 64.2% for in-person. Cancellations were 21.3% versus 29.2%. No-shows were 5.2% versus 6.6%. Adjusted odds ratio for completion with telemedicine was 1.64.
use_in_model: Provides broad evidence that telemedicine can improve appointment completion and reduce cancellations. Use as a general proxy when psychiatry-specific completion benchmarks are limited.
limitation: Not psychiatry-specific. Telephone and video were not separated. General-specialty no-show rates may understate behavioral-health no-show risk.
notes: Useful broad benchmark, but should not be treated as psychiatry-specific.

source_title: No-show management in a forensic outpatient clinic
source_type: Academic thesis / forensic psychiatry outpatient clinic
population_setting: Forensic psychiatric outpatient clinic in the Netherlands
statistic: Initial appointment no-show was 24.9%. Follow-up no-show was 9.8%. The literature review reported psychiatry no-show rates ranging from 10% to 40%, almost double other medical specialties.
use_in_model: Supports baseline assumptions that psychiatry no-shows are material, especially at initial visits.
limitation: Older data. Single European forensic population may differ from general virtual psychiatry patients in the United States.
notes: Useful for broad psychiatry no-show range and first-visit risk framing.

source_title: Therapeutic Alliance and Affordability: Indicators of Early Dropout in Telepsychiatry
population_setting: Retrospective cohort of 796 adults on a national telepsychiatry platform
metric: Early dropout after one or two visits
statistic: Early dropout rate was 13.2%. Authors also note that 20% to 30% of individuals discontinue outpatient mental-health care after one or two sessions in general. Higher patient cost above US$40 per visit and low therapeutic alliance were strongly associated with dropout.
use_in_model: Supports modeling early attrition after first visit and the revenue impact of cost clarity and provider fit.
limitation: Platform population may have selection bias. Dropout definition focuses on early termination and does not capture longer-term attrition.
notes: Strong fit for virtual psychiatry model assumptions.

source_title: Premature Dropout From Psychotherapy: Prevalence, Perceived Reasons and Consequences as Rated by Clinicians
population_setting: Survey of 116 psychotherapists in Europe, with referenced meta-analysis findings
statistic: Therapists estimated average premature dropout at 8.89%, with SD 8.34% and range 0% to 50%. The paper references a meta-analysis finding average dropout of 19.7% across modalities and disorders, and an older meta-analysis reporting unilateral dropout of 47%.
use_in_model: Provides general mental-health dropout ranges when telepsychiatry-specific evidence is limited.
limitation: Self-reported therapist estimates. European sample. Heterogeneous psychotherapy modalities and conditions. Not specific to virtual psychiatry medication management.
notes: Use only as general dropout context, not as a primary Legion assumption.

source_title: Exploratory analysis of electronic patient-reported outcomes collection: comparing online and in-clinic modalities in cancer care
population_setting: 19,340 cancer patients offered electronic patient-reported outcome surveys; 8,556 completed
statistic: Completion rate was 45%. Of completions, 43.3% used in-clinic tablets and the remainder used the online portal.
use_in_model: Provides a lower-bound proxy showing digital intake or patient-reported form completion can be far below 100%. Useful for intake completion sensitivity modeling.
limitation: Not mental-health-specific. Cancer quality-of-life questionnaires differ from psychiatry intake. Completion environment differs from consumer self-serve intake.
notes: Use as conservative lower-bound proxy for digital form completion.

source_title: The Remote Completion Rate of Electronic Patient-Reported Outcome Forms Before Scheduled Clinic Visits
population_setting: Orthopedic clinic emailing PROMIS forms before scheduled visits
statistic: Overall remote completion was 67%. Completion was 74% for new visits, 67% for return visits, and 64% postoperative.
use_in_model: Supports a reasonable benchmark that remote pre-visit forms may reach around two-thirds completion under some conditions.
limitation: Orthopedic population. PROMIS forms differ from psychiatric intake. Patients already had scheduled clinic visits, unlike open consumer intake.
notes: Useful base-case proxy for remote form completion.

category: Digital versus paper patient-reported outcome forms
population_setting: 483 orthopedic patients randomized to tablet or paper surveys
statistic: Overall completion was 84.4%, with no significant difference between digital tablet and paper. Another referenced study reported tablet completion of 98% versus paper completion of 76%.
use_in_model: Shows high completion can occur when surveys are administered in a controlled or required setting. Useful as an optimistic upper-bound proxy for intake completion.
limitation: Not mental-health-specific. Completion was in-clinic and more controlled than self-serve virtual psychiatry intake.
notes: Use as optimistic proxy only, not as a default assumption.

source_title: Quantifying wait times for outpatient mental health services in Massachusetts
source_type: Healthcare access report / Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts Foundation / Abt Associates
population_setting: Surveys of mental-health providers and organizations in Massachusetts
statistic: Among individual licensed providers, 81% of new clients were seen within two weeks of first contact and 93% within one month. Only 7% waited at least five weeks. Among outpatient organizations, 59% were seen within two weeks and 79% within one month; 19% waited at least five weeks.
use_in_model: Provides baseline wait-time context in mental health. Supports the importance of fast scheduling and early availability visibility.
limitation: One state. Pre-COVID. May not reflect virtual psychiatry supply or post-COVID telehealth access.
notes: Useful context for appointment access and scheduling urgency.

source_title: Reducing Appointment No-Shows: Going from Theory to Practice
source_type: Peer-reviewed quality-improvement study / Substance Use and Misuse / PMC
population_setting: Quality-improvement project across 67 addiction treatment organizations
statistic: Reducing wait time from 13 days to 0 days lowered no-show rates from 52% to 18%. Across participating organizations, no-show rates fell from 37.4% to 19.9% after implementing best practices.
use_in_model: Strongly supports the link between faster appointment access and reduced no-shows. Useful for modeling booked-visit-to-completed-visit improvement and activation impact.
limitation: Addiction treatment settings, not virtual psychiatry. Multiple interventions were used, so impact cannot be attributed only to wait-time reduction.
notes: Strong operational benchmark for wait-time and no-show sensitivity.

source_title: Quantifying balance-billing for out-of-network behavioral health care
source_type: Peer-reviewed behavioral-health claims study / Psychiatric Services / PMC
population_setting: National commercial insurance claims from 2011 to 2014 for behavioral-health family-years
statistic: Among families with out-of-network behavioral-health claims, approximately half experienced balance billing. Mean balance bill was US$861, median was US$175, and 90th percentile was US$1,684. High out-of-network cost burden was linked to narrow networks and inaccurate directories.
use_in_model: Supports the argument that cost uncertainty and billing surprise can undermine trust, reduce activation, and contribute to dropout or dissatisfaction.
limitation: Claims data are from 2011 to 2014 and may not reflect current rules such as the No Surprises Act. Out-of-network claims differ from in-network virtual psychiatry workflows.
notes: Use as cost-transparency and trust-risk evidence, not as direct conversion benchmark.
